instruction: How do type constraints work in Go generics? Show practical examples of defining and using constraints.
output:
<think>
Go generics (1.18+) use type constraints to specify what operations are valid on type parameters. A constraint is an interface that can include method sets AND type sets (union of specific types). The constraints package provides common constraints like Ordered (types that support <, >, etc.) and Integer/Float/Complex for numeric types. The comparable constraint is built-in and covers all types that support == and !=. You define custom constraints by creating interfaces with type elements — ~int means "any type whose underlying type is int", which includes type definitions like type UserID int. The tilde (~) is crucial for working with custom types. Without it, your generic function only works with the exact built-in type, not any type derived from it. Constraints can also include methods, combining structural and behavioral requirements. The key design tension in Go generics is simplicity vs power — Go chose a simpler system than Rust's trait bounds, which means some patterns require workarounds.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"cmp"
	"fmt"
	"golang.org/x/exp/constraints"
)

// === BUILT-IN CONSTRAINTS ===

// comparable: supports == and != (built-in)
func Contains[T comparable](slice []T, target T) bool {
	for _, v := range slice {
		if v == target {
			return true
		}
	}
	return false
}

// cmp.Ordered: supports <, >, <=, >= (Go 1.21+)
func Max[T cmp.Ordered](a, b T) T {
	if a > b {
		return a
	}
	return b
}

// === CUSTOM CONSTRAINTS ===

// Type set constraint: only specific types
type Number interface {
	~int | ~int32 | ~int64 | ~float32 | ~float64
}

// ~ means "underlying type" — works with type definitions
type Celsius float64  // Underlying type is float64
type Meters float64

func Sum[T Number](values []T) T {
	var total T
	for _, v := range values {
		total += v
	}
	return total
}

// Without ~: Sum only works with float64, not Celsius
// With ~: Sum works with Celsius, Meters, or any type based on numeric types

// === CONSTRAINT WITH METHODS ===

type Stringer interface {
	String() string
}

type Printable interface {
	comparable
	Stringer
}

func PrintSorted[T Printable](items []T) {
	// Can use == (comparable) and String() (Stringer)
	for _, item := range items {
		fmt.Println(item.String())
	}
}

// === CONSTRAINT COMBINING TYPE SET + METHODS ===

type NumericStringer interface {
	~int | ~float64
	String() string  // Must also have String method
}

// This is restrictive: type must be based on int or float64
// AND have a String() method. Only custom types qualify.

type Score int

func (s Score) String() string {
	return fmt.Sprintf("Score(%d)", s)
}

// Score satisfies NumericStringer: underlying int + has String()

// === PRACTICAL: Generic Map/Filter/Reduce ===

func Map[T any, U any](slice []T, fn func(T) U) []U {
	result := make([]U, len(slice))
	for i, v := range slice {
		result[i] = fn(v)
	}
	return result
}

func Filter[T any](slice []T, pred func(T) bool) []T {
	var result []T
	for _, v := range slice {
		if pred(v) {
			result = append(result, v)
		}
	}
	return result
}

func Reduce[T any, U any](slice []T, initial U, fn func(U, T) U) U {
	acc := initial
	for _, v := range slice {
		acc = fn(acc, v)
	}
	return acc
}

func main() {
	// Built-in constraints
	fmt.Println(Contains([]string{"a", "b", "c"}, "b")) // true
	fmt.Println(Max(3, 7))                                // 7
	fmt.Println(Max("alpha", "beta"))                     // "beta"

	// Custom type with ~ constraint
	temps := []Celsius{20.5, 18.3, 22.1}
	fmt.Println(Sum(temps)) // 60.9 — works because ~float64

	// Map/Filter/Reduce
	nums := []int{1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
	squared := Map(nums, func(n int) int { return n * n })
	evens := Filter(nums, func(n int) bool { return n%2 == 0 })
	sum := Reduce(nums, 0, func(a, b int) int { return a + b })
	fmt.Println(squared) // [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]
	fmt.Println(evens)   // [2, 4]
	fmt.Println(sum)     // 15
}
```

instruction: Build a generic data structure in Go — a type-safe sorted set with proper constraint usage.
output:
<think>
Building a generic sorted set in Go demonstrates several important generic patterns: using cmp.Ordered to constrain elements to orderable types, implementing standard operations (Add, Remove, Contains, Union, Intersection), and maintaining the sorted invariant with binary search. The key design decisions are: store elements in a sorted slice (simple, cache-friendly) vs a balanced tree (better asymptotic complexity). For most use cases, a sorted slice with binary search is faster due to cache locality — binary search on a slice is O(log n) with great cache behavior, while tree traversal has pointer-chasing overhead. The constraint cmp.Ordered ensures elements support comparison operators. We use sort.Search (binary search) for efficient lookup and insertion point finding. The generic Set[T cmp.Ordered] gives us type safety — Set[int], Set[string], Set[float64] are all valid, but Set[[]int] won't compile because slices aren't ordered.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"cmp"
	"fmt"
	"sort"
	"strings"
)

// SortedSet is a generic ordered set backed by a sorted slice.
// T must support comparison operators (int, string, float64, etc.)
type SortedSet[T cmp.Ordered] struct {
	items []T
}

func NewSortedSet[T cmp.Ordered](items ...T) *SortedSet[T] {
	s := &SortedSet[T]{}
	for _, item := range items {
		s.Add(item)
	}
	return s
}

// Add inserts an element, maintaining sorted order. O(log n) search + O(n) insert.
func (s *SortedSet[T]) Add(item T) bool {
	i := s.search(item)
	if i < len(s.items) && s.items[i] == item {
		return false // Already exists
	}
	// Insert at position i
	s.items = append(s.items, *new(T)) // Extend
	copy(s.items[i+1:], s.items[i:])    // Shift right
	s.items[i] = item
	return true
}

// Remove deletes an element. Returns true if it existed.
func (s *SortedSet[T]) Remove(item T) bool {
	i := s.search(item)
	if i >= len(s.items) || s.items[i] != item {
		return false
	}
	s.items = append(s.items[:i], s.items[i+1:]...)
	return true
}

// Contains checks membership. O(log n).
func (s *SortedSet[T]) Contains(item T) bool {
	i := s.search(item)
	return i < len(s.items) && s.items[i] == item
}

func (s *SortedSet[T]) Len() int {
	return len(s.items)
}

func (s *SortedSet[T]) Items() []T {
	result := make([]T, len(s.items))
	copy(result, s.items)
	return result
}

// Union returns elements in either set.
func (s *SortedSet[T]) Union(other *SortedSet[T]) *SortedSet[T] {
	result := NewSortedSet(s.items...)
	for _, item := range other.items {
		result.Add(item)
	}
	return result
}

// Intersection returns elements in both sets. O(n+m) merge.
func (s *SortedSet[T]) Intersection(other *SortedSet[T]) *SortedSet[T] {
	result := &SortedSet[T]{}
	i, j := 0, 0
	for i < len(s.items) && j < len(other.items) {
		switch {
		case s.items[i] < other.items[j]:
			i++
		case s.items[i] > other.items[j]:
			j++
		default:
			result.items = append(result.items, s.items[i])
			i++
			j++
		}
	}
	return result
}

// Difference returns elements in s but not in other.
func (s *SortedSet[T]) Difference(other *SortedSet[T]) *SortedSet[T] {
	result := &SortedSet[T]{}
	for _, item := range s.items {
		if !other.Contains(item) {
			result.items = append(result.items, item)
		}
	}
	return result
}

func (s *SortedSet[T]) String() string {
	parts := make([]string, len(s.items))
	for i, item := range s.items {
		parts[i] = fmt.Sprintf("%v", item)
	}
	return "{" + strings.Join(parts, ", ") + "}"
}

// Binary search: returns index where item is or should be inserted
func (s *SortedSet[T]) search(item T) int {
	return sort.Search(len(s.items), func(i int) bool {
		return s.items[i] >= item
	})
}

func main() {
	// Type-safe: Set[int]
	nums := NewSortedSet(5, 3, 8, 1, 3, 5) // Duplicates removed
	fmt.Println("nums:", nums)               // {1, 3, 5, 8}
	fmt.Println("Contains 3:", nums.Contains(3)) // true

	// Type-safe: Set[string]
	tags := NewSortedSet("go", "rust", "python", "go")
	fmt.Println("tags:", tags) // {go, python, rust}

	// Set operations
	a := NewSortedSet(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
	b := NewSortedSet(3, 4, 5, 6, 7)
	fmt.Println("Union:", a.Union(b))         // {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}
	fmt.Println("Intersection:", a.Intersection(b)) // {3, 4, 5}
	fmt.Println("Difference:", a.Difference(b))     // {1, 2}

	// Compile error: []int is not cmp.Ordered
	// bad := NewSortedSet([]int{1}, []int{2}) // ❌
}
```

instruction: When do Go generics help versus hurt? Show cases where generics improve code and cases where they add unnecessary complexity.
output:
<think>
Go's generics are intentionally simple compared to Rust or TypeScript. They help most when eliminating type-assertion boilerplate, creating type-safe data structures, and writing utility functions (Map, Filter, Contains). They hurt when used for premature abstraction, when the generic code is harder to read than the concrete version, or when the constraint system can't express what you need (forcing interface{} anyway). Rob Pike's guideline applies: don't use generics until you find yourself writing the same function for three or more types. Go's culture favors clarity over DRY — a bit of repetition with clear concrete types is often better than a clever generic abstraction. Specific anti-patterns: wrapping simple operations in generic wrappers that add no safety, using generics when an interface would be simpler, and creating generic types with too many type parameters. The sweet spot is utility functions (Map, Filter, Contains, Min, Max), type-safe containers (Stack, Queue, Set), and avoiding type assertions in data structures.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"cmp"
	"fmt"
)

// === GENERICS HELP: Eliminating repetitive type-specific functions ===

// BEFORE generics: write this for every type
func ContainsInt(slice []int, target int) bool {
	for _, v := range slice {
		if v == target {
			return true
		}
	}
	return false
}

func ContainsString(slice []string, target string) bool {
	for _, v := range slice {
		if v == target {
			return true
		}
	}
	return false
}
// Same function 10 times for 10 types...

// AFTER: One function, type-safe
func Contains[T comparable](slice []T, target T) bool {
	for _, v := range slice {
		if v == target {
			return true
		}
	}
	return false
}

// === GENERICS HELP: Type-safe data structures ===

// BEFORE: Uses interface{}, requires type assertions everywhere
type UnsafeStack struct {
	items []interface{}
}

func (s *UnsafeStack) Push(item interface{}) { s.items = append(s.items, item) }
func (s *UnsafeStack) Pop() interface{} {
	item := s.items[len(s.items)-1]
	s.items = s.items[:len(s.items)-1]
	return item
}

// Must type-assert on every pop — runtime error if wrong:
// val := stack.Pop().(int)  // Panics if it's actually a string!

// AFTER: Compile-time type safety
type Stack[T any] struct {
	items []T
}

func (s *Stack[T]) Push(item T) { s.items = append(s.items, item) }
func (s *Stack[T]) Pop() (T, bool) {
	if len(s.items) == 0 {
		var zero T
		return zero, false
	}
	item := s.items[len(s.items)-1]
	s.items = s.items[:len(s.items)-1]
	return item, true
}

// === GENERICS HURT: Over-abstraction ===

// ❌ BAD: Generic wrapper that adds nothing
func GenericLen[T any](slice []T) int {
	return len(slice) // Just use len() directly!
}

// ❌ BAD: Generic when interface is simpler
type Processor[T any] interface {
	Process(T) error
}
// If T is always the same in practice, just use:
type SimpleProcessor interface {
	Process([]byte) error
}

// ❌ BAD: Too many type parameters — hard to read
func Transform[I any, O any, E error](input I, fn func(I) (O, E)) (O, E) {
	return fn(input) // Just call the function directly!
}

// === GENERICS HELP: Utility functions ===

func Keys[K comparable, V any](m map[K]V) []K {
	keys := make([]K, 0, len(m))
	for k := range m {
		keys = append(keys, k)
	}
	return keys
}

func Values[K comparable, V any](m map[K]V) []V {
	vals := make([]V, 0, len(m))
	for _, v := range m {
		vals = append(vals, v)
	}
	return vals
}

func Clamp[T cmp.Ordered](val, min, max T) T {
	if val < min { return min }
	if val > max { return max }
	return val
}

func main() {
	// ✅ Good generic usage
	fmt.Println(Contains([]int{1, 2, 3}, 2))         // true
	fmt.Println(Contains([]string{"a", "b"}, "c"))    // false

	s := &Stack[int]{}
	s.Push(42)
	val, _ := s.Pop() // val is int, no assertion needed
	fmt.Println(val)

	m := map[string]int{"a": 1, "b": 2}
	fmt.Println(Keys(m))   // [a, b]
	fmt.Println(Values(m)) // [1, 2]

	fmt.Println(Clamp(150, 0, 100)) // 100
	fmt.Println(Clamp(-5, 0, 100))  // 0
}
```

instruction: Show how to use the comparable constraint in Go generics and explain its limitations. What types satisfy comparable?
output:
<think>
The comparable constraint is a built-in constraint in Go generics that allows types supporting == and != operators. It includes: all basic types (int, string, float, bool, complex), pointers, channels, arrays of comparable types, structs where all fields are comparable, and interfaces. Notably, it does NOT include slices, maps, or functions — these can't be compared with ==. The comparable constraint is required for using a type as a map key or in a set-like data structure. There's a subtle distinction: the comparable constraint in generics is stricter than what Go allows at runtime. At runtime, you can compare interface values with ==, but this panics if the dynamic type isn't comparable. The generic comparable constraint prevents this by requiring compile-time comparability. One limitation: if you have a struct with an interface field, it satisfies comparable at compile time, but comparing two instances where the interface holds a non-comparable type (like a slice) panics at runtime.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"fmt"
)

// comparable: the type can be compared with == and !=
// Required for map keys and set-like operations

// === WHAT SATISFIES comparable ===

func Equal[T comparable](a, b T) bool {
	return a == b
}

func main() {
	// ✅ Basic types
	fmt.Println(Equal(42, 42))         // true (int)
	fmt.Println(Equal("go", "rust"))   // false (string)
	fmt.Println(Equal(3.14, 3.14))     // true (float64)
	fmt.Println(Equal(true, false))    // false (bool)

	// ✅ Pointers
	a, b := 1, 1
	fmt.Println(Equal(&a, &a))  // true (same pointer)
	fmt.Println(Equal(&a, &b))  // false (different pointers)

	// ✅ Arrays of comparable types
	fmt.Println(Equal([3]int{1, 2, 3}, [3]int{1, 2, 3}))  // true

	// ✅ Structs with all comparable fields
	type Point struct{ X, Y int }
	fmt.Println(Equal(Point{1, 2}, Point{1, 2}))  // true

	// ❌ Slices — NOT comparable
	// Equal([]int{1}, []int{1})  // Compile error!

	// ❌ Maps — NOT comparable
	// Equal(map[string]int{}, map[string]int{})  // Compile error!

	// ❌ Functions — NOT comparable
	// f := func() {}
	// Equal(f, f)  // Compile error!

	// === GENERIC MAP (requires comparable keys) ===

	type Set[T comparable] struct {
		data map[T]struct{}
	}

	func NewSet[T comparable](items ...T) Set[T] {
		s := Set[T]{data: make(map[T]struct{})}
		for _, item := range items {
			s.data[item] = struct{}{}
		}
		return s
	}

	ints := NewSet(1, 2, 3, 2, 1)
	fmt.Println("Set size:", len(ints.data))  // 3

	strs := NewSet("a", "b", "a")
	fmt.Println("Set size:", len(strs.data))  // 2

	// ❌ Can't make Set of slices
	// bad := NewSet([]int{1})  // Compile error: []int not comparable

	// === LIMITATION: Interface fields can panic at runtime ===

	type Container struct {
		Value interface{} // interface is comparable at compile time
	}

	// Compiles fine:
	c1 := Container{Value: 42}
	c2 := Container{Value: 42}
	fmt.Println(Equal(c1, c2))  // true — int is comparable at runtime

	// But this PANICS:
	// c3 := Container{Value: []int{1, 2}}
	// c4 := Container{Value: []int{1, 2}}
	// Equal(c3, c4)  // Runtime panic: comparing uncomparable type []int

	// comparable guarantees compile-time safety for the TYPE,
	// but interface{} values can hold anything — runtime check only.

	// === PRACTICAL: Deduplicate any comparable slice ===

	func Unique[T comparable](slice []T) []T {
		seen := make(map[T]struct{})
		var result []T
		for _, v := range slice {
			if _, ok := seen[v]; !ok {
				seen[v] = struct{}{}
				result = append(result, v)
			}
		}
		return result
	}

	nums := Unique([]int{3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5, 3})
	fmt.Println(nums)  // [3, 1, 4, 5, 9, 2, 6]

	words := Unique([]string{"go", "is", "go", "great", "is"})
	fmt.Println(words)  // [go, is, great]
}
```
